key_insight: Human experts and LLMs achieved comparable synthesis-recipe success, and their closed-loop collaboration helped discover a new material, Ba3PtO5, with a previously unreported structural prototype.
engineering_relevance: This work shows that AI can accelerate materials synthesis planning when paired with experimental feedback, making it useful for shortening the path from candidate generation to lab validation.
method: Generated synthesis recipes for known and unknown Ruddlesden-Popper materials using humans and LLMs.
Tested all recipes in-lab and fed the outcomes back into a closed-loop collaboration.
Compared success rates across two rounds for known vs. unknown targets to assess learning and adaptation.
evidence_signal: In-lab evaluation showed similar success rates for humans and LLMs on known materials (83% vs 75% in round one; 79% vs 71% in round two) but much lower rates on unknown materials, where collaborative iteration still enabled discovery of Ba3PtO5.
watch_item: Results depend on a specific, well-studied materials family, so performance may not transfer directly to broader chemistries or more complex synthesis spaces.
paper_title: Human and LLM Collaboration for Accelerated Materials Synthesis and Discovery
